core_intuitions: The critic is optional for terminal-reward LLM RL: a small GROUP of sampled responses to the same prompt IS a Monte-Carlo baseline, replacing PPO's learned value network and GAE entirely.

The GRPO advantage is a z-score of the reward within its group. Mean-subtraction is the legitimate variance-reducing baseline; dividing by std makes it scale-free; and every token of a response shares that single scalar (no per-token credit assignment for a terminal reward).

RLOO's leave-one-out baseline excludes R_i (b_i = mean of the OTHER responses) to stay unbiased; GRPO uses the same group baseline but wraps it in PPO's clipped importance ratio so one batch of rollouts can be reused for several gradient epochs.

GRPO's two optimization biases: the per-response 1/|o_i| length normalization gives long correct responses a SMALLER per-token push (and long wrong ones a smaller per-token penalty), and the divide-by-std amplifies low-variance prompts. Both silently inflate response length; the 2025 fixes (token-level loss, drop std, clip-higher) walk GRPO back toward RLOO.

GRPO produces gradient ONLY from mixed-outcome groups. If all G rewards are equal (all correct or all wrong), every advantage is zero and the group is dead, so prompt difficulty must be tuned and dynamic sampling used to keep groups informative.

existing.name: grpo-critic-free-vs-ppo
existing.issue: Renders correctly and lands the punchline (VALUE NET required vs absent) in both themes, but the left column has a minor clutter: the small 'reward' label and 'reward R' box overlap the 'response o' box. On the right, the row 'o_1 o_2 ... o_G  ->  R_1 ... R_G' makes the response->reward correspondence look like two disconnected rows rather than 'each of the G responses gets its own scalar reward'.
existing.improvement: Nudge the left-column 'reward' label / 'reward R' box clear of the 'response o' box so nothing overlaps. On the right, show the G responses and their rewards as an aligned one-to-one mapping (e.g. o_i on the left of a short arrow to R_i, stacked) so it visually reads 'G responses -> G rewards -> mean of the G rewards is the baseline'. Keep the current two-panel PPO-vs-critic-free framing and the 'VALUE NET required/absent' contrast unchanged.

existing.name: grpo-advantage
existing.issue: The concept (reward -> z-score advantage, 'the group is the critic') is exactly right and foundational, but the render has execution bugs. (1) Missing glyphs / tofu boxes: the correct/wrong markers show as empty squares, and the advantage/formula minus signs render as boxes ('R_i [] R', 'R_i [] mean(...)', '[]0.81', '[]1.17', 'push [] above/below mean'). (2) The 'baseline = mean(R)' box overlaps the advantage bar for o_2 (-0.81), colliding into clutter. (3) In dark theme the '<answer>43</answer>' response text overflows outside its rounded response box.
existing.improvement: Replace every non-rendering unicode glyph with render-safe marks: use a filled check and a filled cross drawn as small SVG shapes (or plain 'correct'/'wrong' words) instead of tofu, and use a normal ASCII hyphen-minus (or an SVG minus bar) in 'R_i - R', 'R_i - mean(...)', and the '-0.81'/'-1.17' bar labels and 'push down (below mean)'. Reflow so the 'baseline = mean(R) = the group is the critic, Rbar=0.65' box does not overlap any advantage bar (give the bars their own column to the right, or move the baseline box below the reward column). Clip/shrink the '...<answer>43</answer>' text so it stays inside the response boxes in both themes. Keep the small illustrative rewards (1.2/0.2/1.2/0.0) and the z-score formula banner at the bottom.

new_figures.name: grpo-length-bias
new_figures.concept: Why per-response 1/|o_i| length normalization scales the per-token gradient by 1/length (inflating response length), and how token-level aggregation removes it.
new_figures.rationale: The 'Bias 1' length-normalization argument is the single most subtle and most interview-relevant idea in the chapter, and it is currently pure text + a comparison table. The intuition is inherently visual: it is about the SIZE of the per-token gradient as a function of response length. A picture of arrows-per-token shrinking with length makes the 'cheap to ramble' mechanism click instantly, and directly motivates the token-level fix. This is the chapter's biggest under-served core intuition.
new_figures.placement: Section '### Bias 1: the response-level length normalization' (anchor the marker right after that heading, before or just after the paragraph explaining the 1/|o_i| term).
new_figures.brief: A two-panel infographic contrasting Original GRPO (top) with the token-level fix (bottom). Reading order top-to-bottom. TOP PANEL, titled 'Original GRPO: each response's loss / its own length (1/|o_i|)'. Show four toy responses as horizontal rows of token cells, all with the SAME magnitude advantage symbol (use +A for the two correct rows, -A for the two wrong rows -- symbols only, invent no numbers). Left column labels the response type; right of each row draw one up-arrow (correct, positive advantage) or down-arrow (wrong, negative advantage) PER token cell, where the arrow SIZE encodes the per-token gradient = advantage / length. Rows: (1) SHORT correct, 3 token cells, BIG up-arrows (A/3); (2) LONG correct, 9 token cells, TINY up-arrows (A/9), annotate 'long correct -> weak per-token push'; (3) SHORT wrong, 3 cells, BIG down-arrows (A/3); (4) LONG wrong, 9 cells, TINY down-arrows (A/9), annotate 'long wrong -> weak per-token penalty'. A summary caption under the panel: 'per-token gradient proportional to A / length -> rambling is barely dampened when wrong and barely reinforced-away when right -> response length inflates.' BOTTOM PANEL, titled 'Token-level fix (Dr. GRPO / DAPO): sum over all tokens / total token count'. Show the SAME four responses (same 3 and 9 cell lengths) but now every token cell -- short or long, correct or wrong -- gets an EQUAL-size arrow (up for correct, down for wrong). Caption: 'per-token push independent of length -> no hidden length pressure.' Constraints: use only the small integers 3 and 9 for cell counts; advantage shown as the symbols +A / -A, never invented numeric advantages; encode up/down and correct/wrong with the theme's semantic positive/negative hues (no hardcoded primary colors); everything inside the viewBox with no overlapping arrows; the short-vs-long contrast and the equal-vs-unequal-arrow contrast must be legible at a glance in both light and dark themes. A single static frame fully conveys this.

new_figures.name: grpo-dead-groups
new_figures.concept: GRPO produces gradient only from mixed-outcome groups; all-correct and all-wrong groups have zero advantage everywhere and are 'dead'.
new_figures.rationale: The dead-group / mixed-outcome requirement recurs four times in the chapter (the 'why the critic is optional' resample argument, the pass@k practitioner tip, DAPO dynamic sampling, and the 'all-equal-reward dead group' pitfall warning) and is the practical reason GRPO runs must calibrate prompt difficulty. It is a clean three-case comparison that a static figure nails and that no existing figure shows: readers routinely mis-set difficulty and burn compute on dead groups, so the intuition 'gradient lives only in the variance of the group' is genuinely foundational and currently unillustrated.
new_figures.placement: Callout '!!! tip "Practitioner tip: pass@k as your north star, not pass@1"' at the end of the DeepSeek-R1 recipe section (anchor the marker just before or after that tip); it also serves the later 'all-equal-reward dead group' warning.
new_figures.brief: Three group panels side by side, each showing one prompt with G=4 sampled responses, plus a 'gradient' indicator, to show that only the mixed group trains. For each panel draw the four responses as small tokens tagged correct or wrong (use render-safe marks -- a filled dot / check for correct, a hollow dot / cross for wrong, NOT unicode that may tofu), each with its terminal reward (use only 0 and 1 as illustrative rewards), a horizontal 'group mean' line across the reward column, and the resulting four advantages A_i = R_i - mean drawn as small bars above/below a zero line. PANEL A 'all correct': rewards 1,1,1,1; mean line sits exactly on every reward; all four advantage bars are zero (flat); a gradient meter shown EMPTY; label 'dead group: A_i = 0 for all -> no gradient.' PANEL B 'all wrong': rewards 0,0,0,0; same collapse; advantages all zero; gradient meter EMPTY; label 'dead group.' PANEL C 'mixed': rewards e.g. 1,0,1,0; mean line at 0.5; advantage bars are +/- (two up, two down); gradient meter FULL; label 'informative: mixed outcomes -> real gradient. This is the only group that trains.' Bottom caption spanning all three: 'GRPO learns only from the VARIANCE within a group -> tune prompt difficulty into the mixed band; dynamic sampling discards and refills dead groups.' Constraints: only the illustrative integers 0 and 1 for rewards and G=4; no invented benchmark numbers; correct/wrong and positive/negative advantage encoded with the theme's semantic hues (no hardcoded primaries); the three panels and their empty-vs-full gradient meters must be clearly distinguishable at a glance in both themes, no overlaps, all inside the viewBox. Single static frame suffices.

summary: The chapter is concept-rich and already has three figures. The critic-free-vs-PPO panel and the R1 pipeline render well (pipeline is a clean keep; critic-free needs only minor decluttering of an overlapping reward label and a clearer response->reward mapping). The grpo-advantage figure conveys the right foundational idea (z-score advantage, 'the group is the critic') but has real execution bugs -- tofu/missing glyphs for the correct/wrong marks and every minus sign, a baseline box overlapping an advantage bar, and answer-tag text overflowing its box in dark theme -- so it is a refine, not a replace. Two core intuitions are entirely unillustrated and are the chapter's most subtle, most interview-relevant ideas: (1) why the per-response 1/|o_i| length normalization biases the gradient by length and inflates response length -- an inherently visual arrows-per-token argument -- and (2) the dead-group / mixed-outcome requirement that recurs throughout. I propose one static figure for each (grpo-length-bias, priority 1; grpo-dead-groups, priority 2). No animations: both ideas are single-frame comparisons.
